blocks.text: Chapter 3.
Why You Can't Do Probability
The ideas in 30 seconds
Vivid descriptions make us ignore base rates — the background statistics that should dominate any probability judgment.
Chance has no memory: after 26 blacks in a row at Monte Carlo in 1913, red was no more "due" than on the first spin, yet gamblers lost millions betting otherwise.
We respond emotionally to the size of a possible outcome, not its likelihood — which is why lotteries sell and rare risks terrify.
A plausible story feels more probable than a bare fact, even though adding detail can only make a scenario less likely (the conjunction fallacy).
Human intuition is built for linear change; exponential growth — a paper folded 50 times would reach most of the way to the sun — must be calculated, never guessed.
Our pattern-hungry brains also see clusters in noise, mistake regression to the mean for cause and effect, and irrationally flee unknown probabilities in favor of known ones.
Precise Stories Beat Boring Statistics

Meet Markus: a slim German man who wears glasses and likes listening to Mozart. Is it more likely that he's (a) a truck driver or (b) a professor of literature in Frankfurt? Most people confidently pick the professor — and get it wrong. Germany has roughly 10,000 times more truck drivers than Frankfurt literature professors, so even if only a tiny fraction of truckers love Mozart, they still vastly outnumber the professors.

This is base-rate neglect: the detailed, stereotype-matching description hijacks our judgment, and the underlying frequencies — the base rates — vanish from view. The vividness of the particular crowds out the statistics of the general.

Medical schools fight this bias explicitly. Students are drilled with the maxim "when you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras" — a patient's symptoms almost always indicate the common ailment (a viral infection), not the exotic one, no matter how well the rare disease seems to fit. Doctors who forget this order needless tests and miss obvious diagnoses.

The same error runs through business and investing. A young entrepreneur with a brilliant pitch triggers the "next Google" template, while the base rate — the overwhelming majority of start-ups fail — goes unconsulted. A wine drinker asked to guess a bottle's origin should start not with taste impressions but with production volumes: it's statistically far likelier a random glass is a mass-market French table wine than a rare vintage.

The discipline Dobelli prescribes is mechanical: before judging any individual case, ask what the relevant reference class is and what typically happens in it. Only then adjust for the specifics. Cold statistics should be the anchor; the compelling particulars are merely a small correction — our intuition runs it exactly backward.
The Ball Has No Memory

In the summer of 1913, the roulette ball at the Monte Carlo casino landed on black 26 times in a row. As the streak grew, gamblers stampeded to bet on red, certain the wheel owed them a correction. The casino made millions, because the 27th spin was — as always — a 50/50 proposition. Each spin is an independent event; the wheel keeps no ledger.

This is the gambler's fallacy: the belief that random deviations must balance out in the short run. Flip a fair coin and get 50 heads in a row, and the chance of heads on flip 51 is still exactly 50%. People asked this question routinely answer that tails is now more likely — importing a cosmic sense of fairness into a process that has none.

The fallacy shows up wherever streaks appear. Lottery players avoid last week's winning numbers (or chase them). Parents of three daughters feel a son is "due." Investors assume a stock that has fallen five days straight must bounce.

The crucial skill is telling independent events from interdependent ones. Drawing cards from a deck without replacement does shift the odds — each card removed changes what remains. Weather, too, has memory of a sort: today's conditions influence tomorrow's. But dice, roulette wheels, coins, and lottery balls start fresh every time. When events are truly independent, the past tells you nothing, and any felt sense of "due" is your brain inventing justice where only chance exists.
Magnitude Screams, Probability Whispers

In a classic experiment, researchers told participants they would receive an electric shock and measured their physical anxiety. The striking result: arousal tracked the expected intensity of the shock, not the probability of receiving it. Whether the shock was near-certain or highly unlikely, bodies braced the same way. We respond to the imagined magnitude of an event and remain nearly blind to how likely it is.

Dobelli calls this the neglect of probability, and it drives systematically bad decisions. Lottery marketing works entirely on jackpot size; almost no one recomputes their enthusiasm when odds fall from 1 in 10 million to 1 in 100 million, though rationally the ticket just became ten times worse. Plane crashes and terrorism, being spectacular, provoke fear wildly out of proportion to their tiny probabilities, while statistically deadlier risks like driving barely register.

The bias also distorts how we compare risks. A drop in danger from 5% to 0% feels far more valuable than a drop from 10% to 5%, even though both save the same five percentage points — only zero fully quiets the emotional alarm (this zero-risk bias gets fuller treatment in Chapter 7). Investors similarly sort ventures into "safe" and "risky" buckets rather than grading them along a continuum, funding long shots with huge imagined payoffs while ignoring the odds.

Because we lack any intuitive feel for gradations of likelihood, the only defense is arithmetic. Treat a 0.001% risk and a 1% risk as the thousand-fold difference they are — on paper, since your gut will insist they feel identical.
Why a Good Story Is Less Likely Than a Dull Fact

Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman's most famous demonstration: Linda is 31, single, outspoken, a former philosophy student deeply concerned with discrimination and social justice. Which is more probable — that Linda is (a) a bank teller, or (b) a bank teller active in the feminist movement? Around 85% of respondents chose (b). That answer is logically impossible: every feminist bank teller is also a bank teller, so the subset can never be more probable than the whole set.

This is the conjunction fallacy: adding conditions to a scenario can only shrink its probability, yet added detail makes the story more plausible, and we confuse plausibility with probability. Our intuitive, story-loving mode of thought (Kahneman's fast System 1) evaluates coherence; the slow, effortful System 2 that checks set logic rarely gets consulted.

The effect survives expertise. In another Tversky-Kahneman study, forecasting specialists rated "oil consumption will decrease 30%" as less likely than "a dramatic rise in oil prices will cause consumption to drop 30%" — even though the second statement is strictly a special case of the first. The causal narrative made the compound claim feel more credible than the plain one.

The practical warning: whenever a forecast, pitch, or news analysis wins you over through rich, connected detail, remember that each vivid specific is another condition that must also be true. Detailed scenarios are seductive precisely because they are improbable in a well-crafted way. Strip the story down and ask about the bare event.
Fold the Paper: Exponential Growth Blindness

Fold a sheet of paper in half, then again, fifty times over. How thick is the stack? Intuition offers something like a phone book; the real answer is about 100 million kilometers — roughly two-thirds of the distance from the Earth to the sun. Our minds simply cannot feel exponential growth; evolution equipped us for the linear world of our ancestors, where twice the effort meant twice the berries.

The trap is that small-sounding percentage growth compounds explosively. The essential tool is the rule of 70 (often stated as 72): divide 70 by the annual growth rate to get the doubling time. Inflation of 5% sounds mild until you compute 70 ÷ 5 = 14 — prices double every 14 years, meaning a young adult will see the value of money quartered by middle age. A city growing at 5% a year doubles in 14 years too; a pension fund fee, a traffic count, an epidemic's caseload all obey the same brutal arithmetic.

Dobelli's illustration of how this fools even planners: a politician alarmed that accidents are rising 7% annually should understand that means a doubling every decade — not a gentle drift. Conversely, no growth rate is sustainable forever; anything expanding at a fixed percentage eventually collides with physical limits, which is why ancient Persian kings were bankrupted by the legendary request for rice doubled on each square of a chessboard.

The rule: never trust your gut on percentage growth. When you hear "grows at X% per year," reach for a calculator — or at least divide 70 by X — before deciding whether the number is boring or terrifying.
Three Quick Failures: Clusters, Averages, and Unknown Odds

Clustering illusion. Our brains are pattern-detection machines with the sensitivity dial stuck on maximum, so we find shapes in pure randomness: a face in the rocks of Mars, trends and "support levels" in stock charts, hot streaks in basketball. Genuinely random sequences contain runs and clumps; seeing them proves nothing. When a pattern seems significant, treat it as chance until statistics say otherwise.

Regression to the mean. Extreme performances are usually followed by more ordinary ones, purely because luck contributed to the extreme. Kahneman saw Israeli flight instructors conclude that praise made cadets worse and criticism made them better — when in fact an unusually good landing is simply likely to be followed by an average one, and vice versa. The same illusion credits doctors when back pain (which fluctuates naturally) improves after a visit, and blames coaches when last season's champion slips. Where luck plays any role, expect extremes to drift back toward the average — and don't invent causes for the drift.

Ambiguity aversion. In Daniel Ellsberg's 1961 paradox, people choose to draw from an urn with a known 50/50 mix of red and black balls over an urn with an unknown mix, even when the unknown urn can't be worse on average. We tolerate risk (known probabilities) far better than uncertainty (unknown probabilities) — the Ellsberg paradox. Since most of life's important domains — careers, markets, relationships — offer only uncertainty, an instinct that flees ambiguity systematically steers us away from exactly the decisions that matter most.
In practice
Before judging any specific case, look up the base rate: ask "of all people/companies/situations like this, what fraction turn out that way?" and anchor on that number first.
When you catch yourself thinking an outcome is "due" — in games, markets, or family planning — check whether the events are truly independent; if they are, the streak means nothing.
Put probabilities in writing before deciding about any feared or hoped-for event: multiply the outcome's size by its likelihood instead of reacting to magnitude alone.
When a forecast or pitch persuades you with rich detail, strip it to the bare claim and re-ask how likely that is — every added specific lowers the probability.
Whenever you encounter a growth rate, divide 70 by the percentage to get the doubling time before judging whether the number is small.
Treat surprising patterns and post-extreme rebounds as chance first: demand statistical evidence before crediting a cause, a coach, or a treatment.
